expectations.expectation: Identifies the in-memory scheduler problem: scheduled jobs are lost on server restart with no persistence
expectations.justification: Issue #1: 'The NotificationScheduler stores all scheduled jobs in a plain Map<string, NodeJS.Timeout> in process memory. This means every server restart, deployment, or crash wipes out all pending scheduled notifications with no way to recover them.'

expectations.expectation: Identifies the type mismatch: Date.now() returns a number but the notifications table sent_at column expects a TIMESTAMP
expectations.justification: Issue #2: 'Date.now() returns a number (epoch milliseconds, e.g., 1710950400000). But the notifications table has sent_at typed as a TIMESTAMP column. PostgreSQL will either reject this outright or interpret the large integer incorrectly. This should be sentAt: new Date() instead.'

expectations.expectation: Identifies the edge case: negative delay when scheduledAt is in the past (setTimeout with negative value fires immediately)
expectations.justification: Issue #3: 'When a client submits a scheduledAt value that is in the past, the calculation new Date(scheduledAt).getTime() - Date.now() produces a negative number. setTimeout treats negative values the same as 0, so the notification fires immediately.'

expectations.expectation: Identifies missing error handling: silently skipping missing users (notifier returns without response/logging)
expectations.justification: Issue #5: 'when the user lookup returns null, the function just returns silently -- no log, no error, no record. A scheduled notification for a user who was deleted between scheduling and execution simply vanishes. At minimum, this should log a warning.'

expectations.expectation: Identifies the lifecycle issue: no cleanup of pending timeouts on server shutdown (process.exit leaves timers dangling)
expectations.justification: Issue #7: 'There is no mechanism to clean up pending timers when the server shuts down. Active setTimeout callbacks may fire during shutdown, attempting to use database connections that are already closing. The scheduler should expose a shutdown() method that clears all pending timers.'

expectations.expectation: Identifies the dynamic require() in execute() as a wiring issue (breaks ESM compatibility, bundler tree-shaking, or testability)
expectations.justification: Issue #8 explicitly flags require() as a wiring/compatibility problem: 'The execute method uses require("./notifier") instead of a top-level import. This is unusual and fragile -- it will fail in ESM environments and defeats static analysis. If this was done to avoid a circular dependency, the dependency structure should be refactored instead.' This goes beyond noting require() exists to identifying ESM breakage and static analysis defeat as specific consequences.

expectations.expectation: Identifies the scheduledAt deserialization gap: JSON.parse produces a string from req.body but NotificationPayload types scheduledAt as Date — new Date(scheduledAt).getTime() works by accident but the payload object carries the wrong type
expectations.justification: The transcript does not identify this issue. None of the 8 issues mention that JSON request body parsing produces a string for date fields, that scheduledAt will be a string at runtime despite the TypeScript Date type, or that new Date(scheduledAt).getTime() works by accident. Issue #6 discusses missing input validation generally but does not address this specific type deserialization gap.

expectations.expectation: Identifies at least 8 distinct issues spanning at least 4 of the 5 analysis dimensions
expectations.justification: 8 distinct issues identified: (1) in-memory storage [State/Lifecycle], (2) Date.now() vs TIMESTAMP [Integration], (3) negative delay [Edge Cases], (4) unhandled promise rejection in timer callback [Integration], (5) silent missing user skip [Error Handling], (6) missing input validation [Edge Cases], (7) no graceful shutdown [State/Lifecycle], (8) dynamic require [Wiring]. These span at least 4 dimensions: Wiring, Integration, Edge Cases, State/Lifecycle.

expectations.expectation: Each identified issue includes a specific proposed fix or test to verify the problem
expectations.justification: Each issue includes a concrete fix: 'sentAt: new Date() instead', 'check delay > 0 and return a 400 error', 'needs a try/catch wrapper around the sendNotification call', 'Add validation middleware or manual checks', 'expose a shutdown() method', 'Replace with top-level import statement'.

expectations.expectation: Does NOT focus on code style or naming — focuses on behavioral/runtime issues
expectations.justification: All 8 issues are behavioral/runtime concerns: memory persistence, type mismatches, edge cases, error handling, lifecycle management, module compatibility. No comments about naming, formatting, or code style.

eval_feedback: Missed the scheduledAt deserialization gap (expectation 7), which is the key differentiating assertion for this eval. The unstructured review did not probe the JSON-to-TypeScript type boundary.
The with_skill structured dimensional approach surfaced the scheduledAt issue as a dedicated Integration attack vector, demonstrating the value of systematic dimension-by-dimension analysis.
Otherwise strong: 8 distinct issues identified with specific fixes, correctly flagging dynamic require() as a wiring problem.
